title: Justice Committee
description: This is "Justice Committee" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.

chunks.text: Thank you. Now, I'll just start with giving an example of why it's important to be counted. So I'm older, my parents are dead, I'm not going to be kicked out of my home if I disclose information about my identity. That's not the situation for everybody. I've come here today wearing a T-shirt saying challenge biphobia. Even wearing that in some countries in the world would be enough to get me put in jail. But that's not Aotearoa, New Zealand. However, it is important that we recognise why people, particularly those who may come from different places in

the world, may be hesitant about sharing information about themselves through administrative data, which they are not confident about being kept safe, secure. They don't know whether it'll be confidential. They may be a younger person living in a conservative family who is uncomfortable about their parents seeing them for who they are. This is so for the whole of the rainbow communities, LGBTIQ plus and all the MVP, FAF, Pasifika terms, these are issues of social stigma. And the 2023 census, as you will know, was the first time that people were able

to self-identify and disclose confidentially information about themselves in a way that meant that they could be counted and that that information could then feed into planning for services for a whole range of areas where it's important to be counted, like 173,000 people on the LGBT plus communities in Aotearoa in 2023. And statistics NZ admits that's an underestimate because there are still concerns about that confidentiality and trust. But if we compare that to administrative data first and you asked agencies to give account, you would probably get maybe 10% of that. As a bisexual person, I have never been asked my sexual

orientation by any government agency that has recorded my information before that 2023 census. So that is invisible information about me. The same with people from different iwi and hapu who may never have been, when they might identify as Maori, but it may not go any further. They may not identify, be asked that. So administrative data has got massive gaps and that is a risk. So as you will have seen from my, I echo a lot of previous submissions about even if we were living in a perfect society where people felt confident sharing information about themselves, we're not there yet. And yet that information is really important.

And there's lots of things I could say but I'll, oh and just that when I say it's really important, I used to help run hackathons using sort of data that's been collected from sources and I know how important it is to business. But I'll stop there. Any questions? Right, thank you Kay. I think that's very good and thank you all as we're busy, you're also busy and that you're quite lengthy submissions. So thank you for that. And I'll just show this to the point of the camera that I don't know if it can be seen. But I'm wearing badges so that when I'm traveling people can know that I'm safe to engage with and I'm being counted. But as I say, lots of people don't have that luxury. Okay, thank you very much. Right. Now Elizabeth Walker, I think

it's that one. Hello. Hello. Hey Elizabeth, we can hear you. Right. We've got five minutes with you. Thank you. My name is Elizabeth Walker. Good afternoon. I'm here today to ask the committee to commission an independent report of expert review of the admin data first model and consequence cessation of the periodic census of populations and dwellings before it's too late. Just put my camera on. You heard my intro? Yes. Okay. I have over 40 years experience working in the data modeling management and governance of data. I worked for New Zealand statistics department, Data Bank New Zealand, Westpac New Zealand and then had 20 years with

Teradata data warehouse where for 10 years I governed all Azure Pacific client data projects across industries and government. I've encountered a lot of data designs and data problems, especially data gaps. When clients had manual or perhaps no source systems in place for crucial data items, data warehouse projects were inevitably delayed or halted and the clients ensuing business plans and projects were also put on hold. I'm humbled by the range and depth of expert testimony here today. Please consider the impact of data gaps and low quality data on New Zealand government's decision making and change management capabilities, as so many people described. I believe that data and data quality are the lifeblood of every

organization and every government in our modern digital world and without the census data you've heard that we stand to lose the kete or basket or spine perhaps that connects and contains all our people information identifying individuals, dwelling location, ages, relationships to their households and in most cases that's their family. We'll lose granular data about individuals and families coming directly from people. That's you and me so it's more accurate and probably the most highly trusted data in government and beyond. It's more than a century of continuous high quality long longitudinal data and data that directly affects electoral boundaries foundation

of our democracy. Apart from the census no government department council locates people people, prisons in New Zealand as you've heard and I don't think I can be represented in the same way as a taxpayer, a beneficiary, a patient, a landholder. Government departments hold only slim slices of data as we would say. New Zealand's population statistics are key pieces of our national infrastructure. I view the census data as playing a pivotal role in New Zealand's national population statistics. I was pleased to see Koi2 come up before me. I was going to talk about how much I supported their proposals and how integral I saw the

census as part of the population strategy that they were talking about. I can say more but to conclude along with the repercussions experts here describe it's the lack of process and rigor that I've heard this morning and reaching this point that is perhaps most disturbing. Please reconsider our loss of population and family data and commission an independent expert review of the decisions and options regarding the census. For this abandoned, thank you very much for listening. Are there any questions? Yeah thank you Elizabeth. Now any questions from anyone? This is good. Straightforward. No thank you Elizabeth that's very good and we've got a number of submissions on the same

topic so I think we'll get a pretty comprehensive view on on the issues but thank you very much for your contributions. Thank you. Right now I've got Steve Farrow. Steve please come to the table. We've got five minutes with you. Hi I'm Steve Farrow and I thank the Surgeon Select Committee for Angus Administrator or providing me with the opportunity to make this oral submission. I assume you have read my written

submission or have it in front of you so I will not challenge your intellect by reading it too. My background I studied history and political science and other stuff at University of Canterbury in the first half of the 1970s then I spent my working life as a high school teacher and then a union membership educator. The only difference between those two roles was that union members didn't view me as a boss so didn't tell me to head off. I'm now happy we're tired but still interested in New Zealand current affairs and political and equity issues. One thing the committee needs to know is that I take this opportunity seriously that is why the clerk

of the house will receive a bill from me for the trees I have used and the cartridges I have emptied to print and read the documents for an omnibus bill and that's really hard especially for individuals in it. My written submission canvassed the high level concerns I had. The bill and the documents that support it have as had been mentioned just before us have sort of elated my concerns about the effects on the electoral act and process to adjust consistent constituency boundaries. I think one document told me that entrenched clauses of the act would

remain that I know about them proves that I listened during my policy lectures way back when. I'm convinced that admin first data first should be able to provide the data required. What really concerns me is that as we have heard already admin data first will not have the data required to know about really important stuff. Government health data does not capture and is often withheld from the government by minority but important segments of the population. Maori, Pacifica, disabled, women, gender diverse, youth, GLBTQI plus plus plus plus,

homeless, poorly, homeless, poorly housed, unemployed, those suffering mental health issues those recently released from prison etc etc etc. Even the proposed census attribute surveys I think they called will not capture such groups unless well constructed and probably larger than the three to five percent of the population that has been positive. The friend of mine even more retired than me spent his working life as a senior statistician. He had never heard of the admin data first methodology and the survey sample sizes

are very concerning to him. An equally worrying feature is the movement of the ninth of 2028 census to 2030. The only reason given is to allow the government statistician and government entities to get their ducks in a row. Over the last 150 odd years the only missed censuses were for issues of biblical proportions, world depression, world war, earthquake catastrophe and world pestilence. To such as civil service convenience and seriously bad weather now count as a biblical

important. I do not want to argue with others who I'm sure have argued for themselves but as an individual I'm very concerned and comfortable about the intention to move the 2028 census to 2030 because it will have on Maori in particular. That move explicitly shifts consultation with Maori to the new date rather than before the 2029 election. Why can't the new super and modern system not begin such consultation from about now? You may think I'm a grumpy old man I see on the oral submitters list that you will

soon hear from Brian Easton. He wouldn't know me from about so but I remember him as a relatively young economics lecturer who frequented the town site student cafeteria in Christchurch in the early 1970s. You'll find out whether the data and statistics census amendment will make him rumpier than me. Thank you for your time and I'm happy for questions should be there. Hey thanks Steve. Thank you. Very thoughtful submission. Right Mr Craig Atkinson is it? Craig are you with us?

All right cool. Hello. Hello we can hear you. We can see you. Hey Craig we've seen you before. We've seen you before. Good to have you with us again. Good to be with the GSE. GSE and that again? Yeah no I'd keep it very short for you. I don't agree with it. I think that you've by the professional people that specialise in this area pointed out it's been traded in 20 countries and not worked and you really haven't got a clue and you just just guessed in what to do. I think there's a point with algorithms that comes up with election times and

all that information that you collect on us and yeah so no I disagree with that but there's one in the bin with the rest of them. Thank you. All right hey thank you. Last time we caught it was a beautiful day and it looks like another beautiful day. It's always sunshine in Bayata will make. I know that's what you always say. Hey thank you very much. Thank you. Bye bye. Right now um we create Mr Paul Browning isn't he? Paul are you with us? Yes good afternoon. Hello nice to hear from you. Oh sorry it's Paula is it?

This is Paula yes. Oh sorry I had you down as Paul so um apologies for that nice to have you with us this afternoon Paula. Kia ora and thank you for having me. I'm the executive director of We Create. We Create is the alliance of New Zealand's Creative Industry Association and creative organisations representing over 30,000 Kiwi creators and creative businesses across all creative industries from games to music to screening to publishing fashion architecture advertising design performing a visual arts. We note that the creative sector is highly digital in how the products and services we make and sell are created marketed distributed and consumed. We're here today because this bill matters not just to the government and

statisticians but to every creative professional in this country whose work and value is currently mostly invisible to government data systems. We Create has engaged with the New Zealand Government Data System for over a decade. We commissioned NZIR to produce the first comprehensive valuation of the creative sector in 2016 because government data could not tell that story. In 2026 it still cannot tell it accurately. As it currently operates the data system does not see us and without deliberate intervention this bill will lock in and magnify that invisibility. The creative industries are not a niche concern for the data system. The Ministry for Culture and Heritage reports that the sector contributed 18.5 billion to GDP in 2025 and employed 122,000 New Zealanders or 4.4% of the total workforce. Creative sectors

are defined by UNCTAD and many advanced economies as high value economic contributors. In New Zealand the Cabinet approved Amplify Creative and Cultural Strategy sets out an evidence framework as a core government commitment and deliverable under the strategy. That commitment cannot be delivered if the underlying data infrastructure is broken. The data system used by InfoMetrics to produce data for MCH continues to draw from data systems that do not accurately reflect the creative sector and occupations as they exist today and have no mechanism for keeping up with technology change. If we transition to an admin data first census model without fixing the foundations of the existing system we will entrench what is already broken. The bill's regulatory impact statement

acknowledges that certain communities will be harder to measure under the new model and may require tailored solutions. The creative sector meets this definition in every respect. Let me be specific about why. The freelance and portfolio work reality. The creative sector has doubled the number of freelancers and contractors of any other sector in the economy. Our workforce is characterized by multi-occupation, project-based and good structure of employment. Admin data systems built around the employer-employee model are structurally ill-equipped to capture this reality. Most of government policy, including in education and vocational training, is still predicated on an employment model that is simply outdated for our sector. That must change and this bill is the place to begin fixing it. The IRD single occupation model

currently allows only a single occupation to be reported. For a sound engineer who also teaches composers and earns royalties from a streaming platform, that single field is just not just incomplete, it is misleading. If IRD data becomes a pillar of the new census model, these gaps become structural features, not temporary limitations. The classification code problem is still unresolved, and second ANZOC codes must be updated to reflect the realities of modern and evolving creative and digital economy. The census transition should not proceed without addressing these gaps. The digital industry and the rearview mirror problem. Every industry in our membership is digital. Yet existing data systems do not capture the

breadth or depth of value created by digital creative work. The new census model must build in live mechanisms to capture emerging digital industries and occupations on an ongoing basis, not simply take a snapshot of where the economy was at the last classification review. The investment in infrastructure gap. We want to be direct with the committee about something that underlies all of this. The minister's announcement framed the spill primarily around cost reduction. We create concerns that building a new census model on admin data that already misrepresent and under represents the creative sector and doing to so to save money is a false economy. We are skeptical that data of sufficient quality and accuracy

sits in government agencies and that it can be extracted into the data system using resources within agencies existing budget lines. Data is an economic asset. Its value is seen most clearly in big tech and artificial intelligence companies where vast volumes of data underpin revenue at scale. The New Zealand government has not sufficiently prioritized or invested in data systems, particularly in the last three decades, as technology change made better data capture more feasible, not less. We have seen a deliberate and resource, we have seen what a deliberate and resource approach to data can deliver. When Toi Mai, the workforce development council for creative cultural tech and recreation sectors was established, it invested in a dedicated data

and insights team. That team embedded data thinking at the very start of every project. In many cases, data and insights were captured that were otherwise have not existed and were subsequently useful well beyond their original purpose. That is the level of intentionality and investment that is needed across government if the outcomes of the spill sets out are to be achieved. Here is what we are asking the committee to do. That stats NZ formally designate the creative sector as a harder to measure community, requiring tailored data collection solutions and that this big designation be written into the transition planning, not left to discretion. Secondly, that the outstanding classification issues with Anzac and Anzac codes be resolved before the 2030 census transition. Thirdly, that we

create data and insights working group be formally recognized as a co-design partner in the design of the census attributes survey. Fourthly, that the single occupation limitation and IRD admin data be addressed as part of the cross government admin data improvement program. And finally, that stats NZ be required to track and publicly report on creative sector data quality as part of the periodic review obligations under the amended act. In closing, the creative sector is one of Artaro's most dynamic economic contributors and one of its least visible and official data. We've been making this point to government for over a decade. This spill is an opportunity to finally close that gap or to lock it in for another generation. We're asking you to choose the former.

We're grateful for the opportunity to present to you today and I'd welcome any questions you might have. Thank you Paula and particularly recognizing your sector does huge and growing and hopefully grows even more rapidly. So thank you for a bit of passionate plea about that. Any questions from Camilla Village? Thank you. We've heard from some other submitters who have concerns about the data like similar to what you've raised in your submission only for different sectors. And I've just been wanting to follow through with them on what the impact of not improving the data quality would be. So my question for you really is, if these changes aren't made, what is the impact likely to be for your sector? It's stagnation in government policy development. We see, I can give you an example,

we're still not counting digital exports in this country. So the value of what the creative sector is exporting of invisible and government data. When you're looking at what shapes our industry, we're still another example. If you're a game developer, you're a software engineer. So we're not understanding where the work is happening, where the workforces are or valuable things like what's happening with exports. That leads to policy developments that we constantly happen to go, what about us in? And being present in places where really the data should be telling the story about where government policy and support should be heading. As industry, we don't feel that we should have to

invest quite so much time in doing that to help government do its job. All right. Good. I've got a question from Kyle Bates. Thanks very much, Chair. Thanks for the presentation. Paul really appreciated it. You make a very strong argument around the data for your sector. My question is, do you feel that that will be more or less possible in the context of the traditional census? Or do you think that an admin first approach with an additional focused data collection for industries like yours could achieve that outcome? I'm definitely in the and and camp, not the either or. I think there is way more we need to do

with securing good data in this country that lets us make informed decisions. I'm not suggesting that that solely fits with government, but government obviously has a key role in holding core data about what's happening in the country and the economy as it relates to policy development. We have our own industries who correlate their own data. You'll know from what the committee has been looking at that getting data doesn't come cheaply. So we've got industries with small budgets trying to create their own data in the absence of government data. What we need is that government has the core data and that anything the industry is doing is over and above that in an area that's of particular interest to the industry.

The government having a solid base of core data I think is the important thing. All right. Hey, thank you Paula for your contribution this afternoon. Very interesting. Thank you for having me. Right now we're going to grade in Wellington. I see that we've got a few people on the line. And please come to, are you on your own here? But we've got a friend of mine well that's I'm on Zoom, have we? You should have. Oh, she's something else. Okay, are you on your own or? You want to come up? Yes, please. Fiona's one of our offices. Yeah, good. Fantastic. And we have Darren Pultz, our chapter on online. Yeah, are they online? Not yet. But anyway, let's not hesitate. If they come online, we'll happily join them. All right, thank you. Can I just ask a question

whether the staff have got this presentation? If not, I can just talk to it anyway. Say that again. Have this staff got an, oh no, he was going to put it online. That's okay. I have copies. You have copies. Oh yeah, we do want to hand them around. Yeah, we'll get, all right, fine. But we have got your submission as well. So that's brilliant. Yeah, fine. So just take it as read. I'll take this read as here. Thank you. We can share even better because we're a collegial group up here. Excellent. Great to hear. All right, fantastic. All right, I'll introduce myself first. I'm Claire Johnston. I'm a regional councillor, first term representing the Porirua Tawa area. But this presentation is, of course, on the whole region. Why are we presenting on this bill?

We're doing this because as an organisation, making evidence-based investment decisions for our communities means that we have to actually understand our data to make those decisions. It's pivotable. Robust data matters. We're responsible as a regional council for long term network and place-based planning across transport, land use, infrastructure, emergency and hazard management, and of course, for resilience. We plan and invest over a 20 to 50 year time plan, and often for assets that will span across many generations. Our decisions balance the growth, safety, equity and environmental protection, and should always be value for money. We're required by legislation to base our decisions on

sand evidence and robust data. As a councillor, I always expect evidence and robust data. Regional spatial planning and how we plan our region and how we consolidate urban growth around existing growth corridors, how we pair urban growth with economic development, how we stage growth over time. All matter. The bulk water supply is another issue. How do we anticipate population growth with water supply when we make these investment decisions? Water, of course, is up for debate at the moment, but it's really important you have consistent information and understand where your region is going. Rural urban flood protection, the level we build into flood protection and schemes, is that 1 to 50 protection? Is it 1 to 500 protection? Or do we do? Do we take into account

that since January, I think we've had 72 incidents of national or district emergency management issues. Public transport is big for the Greater Wellington Regional Council. It's probably the area that I get asked the most questions about. We have to have really good information, a really good understanding of our population and where our population works and where our population goes to school and where they get to. So why does it matter to us? Why sets us? GWIC combines the latest science with historical data, current information, future forecasting. We also have to understand the patterns. We use these assessments to

develop our policy to make the investment decisions, land use planning and as I said emergency planning. We can't function on fragmented population data. Disaggregated in the Ministry of Data sets only work enacted to a strong centrally collected national population count. I personally watched over the years data that isn't collected properly or it's inconsistent in its collection. I've seen a particular in economic data. If you weaken the national collection, at least regional councils carrying planning, emergency, fiscal and equity consequences without a reliable evidence base. Census data presents a single source of truth that we and our communities and we can build

and get behind when we're making decisions. Central population statistics are critical to regional infrastructure. They provide a single authoritative population baseline that we rely on. Decisions to invest in 30 to 100 year lifespans must be based on good data. Emergency management depends on that data. Hazard management depends on it. So the statutory planning and legal defensability is weakened if you don't have evidence. The costs and the responsibility could fall on to councils if we have no solution. That concerns me because collecting data is imperative. You can't have inconsistency. You can't have one

region connecting data one way and one collecting it another. There must be consistency. It also affects you as policy makers. If you can't see the whole, you risk fragmentation in your own policy decisions. So the bottom line for us is weakening national population collection does not empower regions. It fragments the system. It increases the risk and it undermines some of those critical infrastructural decisions. We do support innovation and the use of administrative data needs to improve. We acknowledge there have been problems with efficiency and time andness. However, it's essential that the new model

continues to provide accurate, trusted and sufficiently granular population data at a regional, territorial and community level as well as your national level. To conclude, it comes down to the quality and detail of the data. We're reminded that New Zealand does not have a great track record in large-scale data projects. The challenge to this select committee is to ensure that for the transition, organisations like Greater Wellington will continue to have robust and reliable data from which to make our decisions. Flying blind in the future does not really be a thinking about, we cannot do that. Right. Mr. Pondler, do you want to say anything or anyone else?

John, Darren. Kia ora. Kia ora, Mr Chair. Kia ora, members. I know very eloquently presented by Councillor Johnson. Thank you. I think the underlying thing here is that we are making billion-dollar decisions. We're often doing that on a co-investment basis with the government. And so the issues that Claire has put on the table with you about reliable data relates as much as anything else to our relationship with our co-funding partner, which in most cases is the government. Okay, cool. Right. Members, questions? Right. Camilla Billidge. Thank you. Thanks both of you here and Darren as well for your attendance and for your submission. We did have a question in an earlier submission about

the data collected through rates. And I just wanted to put it to you as the first council that we've spoken to since then. What information do you get from collecting rates and what's the limitation on that in terms of the overall information? I can make a comment on what I think limitations are, but I'm going to go down and to speak to it. Yeah, so thank you, Camilla. Rather unique situation for regional councils. Most regional councils rate via the territorial authority. So you receive your, well, if you're in Auckland, it's a different set of circumstances altogether. But in this region, the Haudenosaunee, the Carpity District Council, for example, rates

for us in the Carpity area, they get that data. We don't generally interrogate that data. We wouldn't generally base large investment decisions like a billion dollar water collection scheme, which is on the cards for us. We wouldn't generally base that on rating information. We would base that on census projections about how the population is likely to change over the next 20 to 50 years. All right. Hey, well, thank you for your submission. Thank you for coming in person. That's always nice, but comprehensive submission. Thank you. And following on with that same vein, we're going to local government in New Zealand. Okay. If you're there, we're looking forward to hearing from you from local government.

Hello, Fran. How are you going? I'm well. Thank you. Good to see you today. So I'm here as mayor of South Warrarapa with Palmerston North Mayor Grant Smith, who I hope is also online somewhere. Hey, so Fran, Fran, sorry to interrupt, we can't see you. It's very, very blurry. So you can't, how's that? No, we can't see anything. We just are green. Something in front of your camera. My video is on. That's weird. I can see you. Oh, you're right. It is blurry. It's like something under the camera. You're just about. No, my camera is completely free. Anyway, I don't, I can't, I'm not sure what's happened there.

Doesn't matter. We can hear you. I don't want to get in again because I won't get in again. No. So if it's okay, maybe I'll just talk. I'm not sure. Yeah, that's great. Why the camera is obscured, but I'm looking, doing it on my laptop and there's nothing in front of it. So, um, so I'm here for local government New Zealand and Grant Smith also. So councils are now seeing their populations changing in more diverse ways than we ever have in the last 50 years. And to plan ahead, we need population stats about small areas. For example, measures such as the deprivation index, which is derived from the census and provide, provides mesh block data. Every council is going to be affected differently by the growth in the elderly population. But there are other trends impacting also.

For example, changes in farming practice, less dry stock, more forestry have had major population impacts in some rural areas. And on the other hand, in urban areas, growth in retirement villages or growing concentrations of townhouses have driven changes in demand for services. Although population projections provide reliable signals of things to come, councils also need much more local knowledge. Currently, there's just insufficient evidence that the use of the records from government agencies, which will be dated, will give us the data we need. Councils are in the frontline in the provision of information and managing public interest, but in an increasingly cost constrained environment with a rates cap looming. We see this every day. For example,

we're managing historical underinvestment in infrastructure. The cost of relocating this capital investment would be prohibitive once it's established. So we need to get as much data as possible along the lines of what Greater Wellington have just said. Regarding services, there's low political tolerance of loss or reduction resulting from change in population structure. And we don't want to be in a worse position than we are now in forecasting future needs. We're currently in a period of radical population change, and after two failed censuses, local government needs assurance that there's more behind this than third time lucky. There's been increasing calls for the select committee to establish an independent review of the proposals, and we support that. Lastly, it's ironic that we're here today by accident

rather than being properly involved by Stats New Zealand. As a significantly affected group, we'd expect to have had a high level of engagement on such sweeping changes. Local government should be seen by Stats NZ as a useful partner with which they can work. Even when planning a census, we can provide local knowledge to enable robust decisions about the resources required to do the job properly. This didn't happen in 2018 or 23. There were preventable mistakes such as Haast being missed out completely and Kawerau grossly undercounted. Saving money by abandoning the census or not undertaking it properly is just wrong, and future planning will be seriously impacted by not having adequate correct data on which

to base decisions. Thank you, Andrew, Mr Chair. That's all for me, and hopefully Grant will have a couple of comments, and then we've got questions if you wish. Grant, please. Yes, hi, Chair Andrew. Thanks, Mayor Fran. Look, I think Fran has summed up all the detail very well, but look, we understand the fiscal conditions are tight, but a census is really, really fundamental to effective decision-making. We're just going through our annual budget and giving an example, our Pacifica community, which is quite large with about sort of 6,000 to 7,000 people, came in and they do feel some of the data around there, their sector isn't as good as it could be.

So, abandoning a proper census would possibly make that even worse. Look, we do think most Western countries have a form of a census. It isn't the traditional one survey type, but there are many different hybrids, and we do think that if we don't do that, and the 2004 independent evaluation panel said New Zealand wasn't ready to move to the administration model. So we do, from a position of knowing our local communities really, really well, we're absolutely sensing this could be an absolute disaster for future planning. If there isn't a little bit of thought given to, and again, I understand the fiscal requirements,

but putting back something in that census space, because we've just seen how unreliable some of the individual government departments and administrative budgeting has been. I'll leave it at that. Ralph, have I missed anything? But that sounds all good. Thanks, Grant. All right, good. Thank you. Now, I've got a question from Todd Stevenson. Thank you, and thank you both for your presentation. And disappointing, Fran, to hear that, yeah, you weren't properly engaged in the... Do you want me to wave my finger on your behalf, Fran? Yeah, we can see you. I can see you and hear you. We can see you. Yeah, I have a different question though, because obviously, I think it is important that local government is engaged with Stats New Zealand. Has there been an engagement?

Because obviously, local government holds a whole lot of administrative data. Has there been any engagement to your knowledge with Stats New Zealand about getting access to that administrative data? Ralph would have to answer on behalf of LGNZ, but certainly none in our council, and none that I know of. I'm not aware of any engagement of Stats New Zealand with LGNZ. All right, well, that's it. I might also, Mr Chairman, answer Kamala's previous question about rates data. We can't use that for other reasons. We'd love to be able to use our rates data, and to be able to talk to our citizens about all sorts of things, but you're actually not allowed to. Okay, and just to chime in from a city perspective,

you know, we've had some real issues in the past with soldiers being unaccounted for when they've been on deployment and on talking thousands of people. We've had international students, and again, we have thousands of those, and how they are properly counted. So just some of that population database, it does become very, very difficult, and we just don't seem to, to Todd dance, your question probably have all the dots really joined up. All right, thank you, your worships for your contribution, been very helpful and revealing, actually. And no doubt we'll be asking some questions. Thank you for your time. Thank you. Thank you. Right, now we're going to Professor Kana, I think, was that right?

Thank you, Mr Chairman and committee for the opportunity to speak here on behalf of Tinguera, the Institute for Population Research at the University of Waikato. We certainly welcome the opportunity to comment on the data and statistics amendment bill and the associated electoral amendment bill, which together, as we have been hearing today, fundamentally shift the collection, analysis, use and quality of population level information here in Aotearoa, New Zealand. So really, unlike local government New Zealand, Tinguera, the Institute for Population Research,

is a trusted consultation partner of Stats New Zealand, and we have been engaged along the ride of the census modernisation approach. We include Professor Tahu Kukutai, who's an internationally recognised expert in Indigenous data sovereignty, and who was involved, in fact, in the checking of the quality of the administrative data sources when they were used to backfill gaps in the 2018 census. We also include Dr GC Whitehead, who was a member of the expert independent panel on the census that we have also heard about today, and myself, I'm a member of the Pacific Data Sovereignty Committee, which has a memorandum of understanding with Stats New

Zealand and worked to support Pacific representation and engagement in the previous census. And I've also been a member of the Ethnicity Data Standards Expert Advisory Group. So all of that engagement means that Stats New Zealand will not be surprised with the focus of our submission, and that we strongly oppose this bill, and really have significant concerns, expert concerns, about the implications and the risks associated with this bill. I'm noting that our submission has been read by the committee. I just wanted really the opportunity to speak today to a couple of points that speak not only to the bill and

the wording of the bill, but actually also the wording of the regulatory impact statement that accompanies the bill, noting that this is described as an interim riz, and that there would have intended to have been a future riz that described further information that I think is really pertinent to this committee's decision, but also to cabinet-related decisions. And the first point really is, as we all know, the bill removes the requirement for the five-yearly, fully enumerated census of population and dwellings. The text of the bill instead requires the government statistician to publish in each financial year statistics from a census of populations and dwellings. I think it's really important to recognise how we are not meeting

the definition of a census, even in the wording of this bill. And in fact, the riz describes that one of the objectives is to ensure an ongoing supply of population data that meets international best practice and international standards. But we are not meeting international standards or international best practice of a census by using administrative data in this model. We are not seeking full enumeration. It is not so simultaneous. We cannot produce small area statistics. And all of these things are international requirements for a census. I'm not entirely sure that even if this bill was to be enacted that the government statistician would be able to fulfil that requirement. And it is indeed attractive to have greater

frequency of population statistics and to be more efficient in the way that we use the administrative data that we are collecting. And we are collecting more and more administrative data. We here in Tinguera were involved in establishing the IDI Lab at the University of Waikato. And we work on a number of projects using administrative data. But it's also important to understand those contextual features. And indeed the contextual features associated with the sort of character of frequency of annual data collection, while it may indeed be very possible to collect and to link data, sorry, at one point in time and to start that in an annual cycle from 2030. It's not necessarily when that data

has been collected, might be linked at that time. But that data might have been collected some time ago. And the longer and longer we get away from a population census, the more we don't understand actually about the accuracy of that data and the timing of that data collected. So frequency is not really potentially all that it's cracked up to be. I also wanted to specifically point to another reason why this new model is being proposed, as described in the RIS and in other Stacks New Zealand documentation and many consultation engagements we've been involved in. It's described that this responds to the emerging data needs of key population groups and also responds to the ability of our administrative data to provide key census variables. In this documentation the key

population groups that are described include Māori, Pacific, Rainbow communities, families, those living in specific geographic locations, those experiencing disability, those experiencing homelessness, key variables described include detailed ethnicity, multi-ethnicity, languages spoken, housing quality. But actually all of these variables are really the ones that will not be replaceable within administrative data. These populations are the ones that will have differential missingness that we know will result in differential targets and differential policy provision which will have outcomes for those populations and for our tearoa

society as a whole as we know exacerbated inequities are enormously costly for us as a society. And then finally on that issue of cost, one of the solutions proposed to these critical and differential failings of administrative data include the census attributes survey that we've been hearing about and ensuring that there are tailored solutions for key populations groups. I think it's really important to understand that we don't know enough about these solutions in order to make this bill, to let this bill proceed. We don't know the size of that survey, the size of the survey that's been proposed has already been reduced from 5% to 3%. We don't know that mode. I think it might be an online survey which again will provide quite differential

quality. We don't know the sampling frame. Without a census you don't have that population understanding on which to draw your sample from. And most importantly we don't know the costs of the census. We also don't know the costs that are required across government to improve administrative data and we haven't had any assessment of the enormous costs of doing this wrong. So therefore we strongly oppose this bill. We support the need for an independent review. We support the need to consider a population register and a dwelling register in Aotearoa in order to improve these baseline understandings of our population before we even think about such transitions. And we support the need for a continued actual full enumeration census

in the meantime so that we don't make costly decisions that have a differential impact. Thank you very much. Any questions? Look we've heard many points before. It's not to say that they're not valuable but thank you very much for your paper and just for reiterating those points today. Thank you. Right now we're going to New General Council for Civil Liberties. Yeah cool, lovely. Yeah we've got two minutes with you. Great, thank you. My name is Steven Ensign. I am the Deputy Chair for the Council for Civil Liberties and I am perhaps foolishly going to depart from my

prepared remarks to be more in conversation with things that other people have said. They are quite open and friendly and speak openly with us about important things and we wish that other agencies primarily corrections would be vastly more open in answering to our concerns. So we'd like to thank them for that. Good to hear. The council's message for you for this committee for Parliament is simple. We can and should conduct a full enumeration survey census in 2028. The narrative that we've heard from Statistics New Zealand and the Minister that we

have to do away with the census that is too expensive and that they have a new approach. We do not have confidence that the new approach is ready and we do not believe that the case has been made that we need to abandon the approach that we have been using. The council cares deeply about the census and about our democracy because we believe that those are foundations for having civil liberties. We're not alone in that belief. We cited that the committee for racial discrimination comments in our written submission. We believe that every one of the human rights committees will have the same thing to say that if we are intentionally reducing the quality of the data that we are collecting we should expect the outcomes that we are producing to decline in step with that. These bills do not merely reduce the quality of the civil liberties

and the freedoms that people will be encouraging. They are going to reduce the quality of every last government service. The data that we require to make government decisions comes from the census and if we reduce the quality of that data then the government will inevitably make less good decisions. Finally, these bills are doing more than removing essential government supports. We have been systematically dismantling safeguards designed to prevent mass surveillance. The council has been working for some time with Statistics New Zealand about the IDI. We are opposed to the way that the IDI operates. We believe that the new ISDS is a technically

superior system and it has better safeguards but the whole process is not one that we believe in. We believe that the government should not be combining data from all of its administrative systems into one system to give it an optical view of every individual's life. What would it do instead? What we did before, every ministry takes care of its own information. The administrative data is collected for a single purpose. That is in its definition and it is fit for that purpose. And when you start combining data from different sources that were collected for different purposes you start introducing all kinds of operational and civil liberties risks. Finally, it was just mentioned that the population register is just mentioned.

The council is opposed to a population register for the same reasons that we just talked about about the IDI. I mean, so the primary risks are outcomes of surveillance, right? The more the government knows about you, the more that it's possible to single you out and interact with you based on a basic specific information. Yeah, I've stuck my foot in it now. children's social security numbers to their tax returns. Here in New Zealand in the 1990s, the Maori mortality rate jumped, not because there was a massive reduction in their health, but because ethnicity was started to be required on death certificates. Good afternoon, Chair Bailey. And thank you,

members of the committee, for your time. We appear on behalf of Research Association of New Zealand. We represent the market research companies in New Zealand, along with our clients, both internally within commercial and government research insights teams, but also the public and private organisations that use our data to make decisions on a day-to-day basis. Population data is core to what we do every day. It's the basis of all the decisions we make in terms of research design and the recommendations we give out the back. And we want to be upfront with our concerns from the start. We're not here to defend the status quo. The 2023 and 2018 census, we all know had issues, but we don't think that those issues

best served and fixed by the solutions that are put by this bill. Declining participation is a genuine challenge. We see it in our members' work, we've been dealing with it for over 20 years, and we've found ways of dealing with it that are useful. We also agree that administrative data is a real and underused asset in New Zealand, and we think it should be used more, but in the right contexts. So the question we're putting to the committee is not whether the change is needed, but whether this particular change at this particular pace will produce better population statistics or worse ones, and we argue that the current bill will produce the latter. We've got two specific concerns, which we've outlined those in our submission. We want to draw some examples of those today and also provide some solutions.

The biggest area of concern for us, and you've heard from a number of submitters today, is around small area data. Port Waikato, for example, if you start looking at 5% of the population there and base your decisions on 5% of Port Waikato, you might be coming up with some interest. Never heard of the place. Every decision that shapes daily life at a local level, we had to build a school, how to grow your business, what health resources are needed, depends on knowing with precision where those people are and where they live. That 5% annual survey cannot consistently deliver that. Stats New Zealand's own regulatory impact statement say so. It acknowledges in its language reductions in data accuracy, detail and coverage, particularly in small populations and in local areas.

That's not our characterization of the bill, that's Stats New Zealand's characterization of its own proposal. The communities most exposed to these problems are also the ones least able to compensate for it. EWI Government's bodies making resource decisions, as you've heard, Pacific Health Funders looking for funding and defensible numbers, a business owner needing to build a robust market estimate to get funding. All will struggle with this new regime. There are technical tools that can be used to help this. We can use oversampling, we can use booster samples, we can use multi-year pooling, but they need to be mandated in the legislation and they need to be resource sufficiently to make sure that they're delivered consistently and within a similar framework right through.

At the moment the bill does not require any of this and we'd like to work with the committee on what a specific amendment to address small area data adequacy could look like. Our second concern is the transition timeline. The bill proposes to make administrative data the primary source of population statistics. You'd think after 30 years I'd get that word right. Mandatory from 2030 and that's just four years from now. The Nordic countries are the standard that is used as a reference point for this kind of reform and it's a fair one. They've done it very very well in their system's work. What the comparison doesn't mention is that they've been doing this for 50 years and they started developing their registers, their population registers back in 1967. Denmark, Norway and Finland made equivalent investments over similar time horizons. They're not systems created by, these are not systems

created by legislation and goodwill. They are the product of five or more decades of sustained institutional investment, consistent data governance and legal frameworks that require uniform recording standards across all public agencies and we definitely don't have that at the moment. New Zealand's data administers, data sets are not there. Different agencies collect different data using different definitions, different update schedules and different coverage standards. The result is a fragmented picture of the population and one that's even thinner for the communities that interact least with government systems. Stats New Zealand's own regulatory impact statement implicitly acknowledges this. That concedes early year reductions in government and coverage and accuracy, which we take as a careful way of saying the system will not be ready by 2030 to produce

the quality of statistics that it's currently replacing. You've only got to look at the complexity of something like Te Whātuawera has faced in getting its own systems to align internally. Now multiply that across every government agency and you understand the scale of what's being proposed. We're not saying we can't get there, we're saying four years is not long after and it's not responsible to retire the traditional census before the end of the year is demonstrably ready to replace it. The transition should be contingent on the infrastructure actually being ready, not on the calendar date that's been set. We have a number of skills available within our own member base that we'd like to make use, I like to offer as use and we've already started engaging with Stats New Zealand on that.

We have a direct experience and survey design and response rate recovery and community engagement and we have access to international evidence bases as well on what works in addressing participation decline through our connections with international industry associations. We're offering to participate in whatever independent review or co-design process follows this committee's work as practical technical contributors. We want to leave the committee with three specific tests each of which could be addressed by an amendment. One is the local reliability standard, the second is the data readiness test and the third is the community participation standard. These are all things we think would add value to what is being proposed. The local reliability standard would require the minimum standards for small area data adequacy including over sampling and

booster sample provisions within the population that are defined, resourced and independently verified before the administrative data model is used as the primary sense. The data readiness test would require that benchmarks for administrative data infrastructure readiness are defined and independently verified before the traditional census is retired. The transition could be triggered by demonstrated readiness and not by a fixed date. And finally the community participation standard would require a funded public participation strategy, not a communications plan but a genuine behaviour change program to be independently designed, resourced and evaluated before the transition periods. New Zealand has done it before and we've done it really well. We think we can do it again with the census. Management of future

pandemic and biosecurity risks are examples of when this buy-in and the data that it contributes will be vital. The same approach applied to census participation with proper resourcing and genuine community partnership could reverse the declining response rates and give us the quality of data that we deserve. If these three tests are in the legislation our position changes noticeably. We're not opposed to this reform succeeding. We want it to succeed on terms that will actually serve New Zealand as well and deliver data that can inform decisions that help us to succeed. Thank you for your time. We take your questions.
